label: verify-sacramento-newspaper-guild-v-sacramento-
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:
## Authority as cited (variants seen in the documents)

- Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd

## How the documents used it

[delphi] ….4th 363 (2)
- *Frazer v. Dixon Unified School Dist.* (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 781 (2)

**Cited by only one panelist:**
- Cal. Const. art. I, § 3(b) (Prop 59)
- Gov. Code §§ 54960 and 54960.5 — standing and fee-shifting
- *Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd. of Supervisors* (1968) 263 Cal.App.2d 41
- *Common Cause v. Stirling* (1983) 147 Cal.App.3d 518 (cited tentatively)
- 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 239 (2002) (cited tentatively)
- Legislative history of the 1993–94 amendments …

[tetlock] … whether the eventual public 'action' is the product of antecedent private deliberation, not just whether the vote itself was held in open session (substance-over-form; see the foundational 'rolling quorum' rationale in Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd. of Supervisors (1968) 263 Cal.App.2d 41 — verify continued vitality).
- Whether a §54952.2 serial-meeting violation is independently reachable via §54960.1 depends on treating it as a violation of §54953's open-meeting…

[naive_fable] …"** or **"deliberate"** — no concurrence, agreement, or vote is required.
- "Deliberation" is construed very broadly: it includes "the collective acquisition and exchange of facts preliminary to the ultimate decision." *Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd. of Supervisors* (1968) 263 Cal.App.2d 41; *Frazer v. Dixon Unified School Dist.* (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 781.
- The classic condemned structures are the "daisy chain" (A→B→C) and the "hub and spoke" (staffer or intermedi…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q1 — The AI assistant as serial-meeting intermediary (Brown Act).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"VERIFIED","canonical_cite":"Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd. of Supervisors (1968) 263 Cal.App.2d 41","note":"Case and cite confirmed exactly as given (3d DCA, 1968, 263 Cal.App.2d 41). It genuinely holds that \"deliberation\" under the Brown Act includes \"the collective acquisition and exchange of facts preliminary to the ultimate decision,\" directly supporting naive_fable's quoted use. The [tetlock] characterization of it as the \"foundational 'rolling quorum' rationale\" is somewhat of a stretch — the case's facts involve a single gathering with an actual quorum present, not a serial chain; the explicit telephone-chain/rolling-quorum language is more precisely from Roberts v. City of Palmdale (1993) 5 Cal.4th 363, 381, which built on this case's broad deliberation definition. Not enough to fail the citation, since it's flagged with a hedge in the source doc, but worth noting as an imprecise attribution rather than a false one.","source_urls":["https://law.justia.com/cases/california/court-of-appeal/2d/263/41.html","https://caselaw.findlaw.com/ca-court-of-appeal/1818917.html","https://scocal.stanford.edu/opinion/roberts-v-city-palmdale-31514","https://law.justia.com/cases/california/supreme-court/4th/5/363.html"]}

data.canonical_cite: Sacramento Newspaper Guild v. Sacramento County Bd. of Supervisors (1968) 263 Cal.App.2d 41
data.note: Case and cite confirmed exactly as given (3d DCA, 1968, 263 Cal.App.2d 41). It genuinely holds that "deliberation" under the Brown Act includes "the collective acquisition and exchange of facts preliminary to the ultimate decision," directly supporting naive_fable's quoted use. The [tetlock] characterization of it as the "foundational 'rolling quorum' rationale" is somewhat of a stretch — the case's facts involve a single gathering with an actual quorum present, not a serial chain; the explicit telephone-chain/rolling-quorum language is more precisely from Roberts v. City of Palmdale (1993) 5 Cal.4th 363, 381, which built on this case's broad deliberation definition. Not enough to fail the citation, since it's flagged with a hedge in the source doc, but worth noting as an imprecise attribution rather than a false one.
